notes: literal ill vs slang impressive or excellent
examples: He felt sick after the roller coaster.
He felt sick after the roller coaster.
literal
That skateboard trick was sick.
That skateboard trick was impressive.
slang

notes: literal burning/hot vs slang excellent or exciting
examples: The building was on fire.
The building was on fire.
literal
This new album is fire.
This new album is excellent.
slang

notes: literal past of light or illuminated vs slang fun/intense or intoxicated
examples: He lit the candle before dinner.
He lit the candle before dinner.
literal
The party last night was lit.
The party last night was very lively and fun.
slang

notes: literal no longer alive vs slang very quiet or unresponsive (e.g., a room or phone)
examples: The plant is dead from lack of water.
The plant is dead from lack of water.
literal
My phone was dead at the concert.
My phone had no battery at the concert.
slang

notes: literal strike/rhythm vs slang exhausted or defeated
examples: She beat the drum in time with the music.
She beat the drum in time with the music.
literal
After the hike he was beat.
After the hike he was exhausted.
slang

notes: literal physically broken or past tense of break vs slang having no money
examples: The vase is broke and needs glue.
The vase is broke and needs glue.
literal
I can't go out, I'm broke.
I can't go out, I have no money.
slang

notes: literal spirit vs slang stop responding or cut off contact
examples: They say the house is haunted by a ghost.
They say the house is haunted by a ghost.
literal
He ghosted me after the third date.
He stopped responding to me after the third date.
slang

notes: literal tasting of salt vs slang resentful or annoyed
examples: The soup is too salty.
The soup is too salty.
literal
She was salty about losing the game.
She was annoyed about losing the game.
slang

notes: literal moderately cold vs slang good, fashionable, or approving
examples: Open the window, it's not cool in here.
Open the window, it's not cool in here.
literal
Those shoes are cool.
Those shoes are stylish.
slang

notes: literal garbage vs slang worthless or very bad
examples: Put the trash in the bin.
Put the trash in the bin.
literal
That essay is trash.
That essay is of very poor quality.
slang

notes: literal spherical object or formal dance vs slang to spend money freely or to have a great time
examples: She kicked the ball into the goal.
She kicked the ball into the goal.
literal
He likes to ball when he gets paid.
He likes to spend a lot of money when he gets paid.
slang

notes: literal playing-card or expert vs slang to do extremely well (a test or task)
examples: He drew an ace from the deck.
He drew an ace from the deck.
literal
She aced the math exam.
She did extremely well on the math exam.
slang

notes: literal electrical connector vs slang a supplier or contact for goods/services
examples: Plug the charger into the outlet.
Plug the charger into the outlet.
literal
He's my plug for concert tickets.
He is my contact who gets me concert tickets.
slang

notes: literal hat or cover vs slang a lie (as in 'no cap' meaning no lie)
examples: He put a cap on the marker.
He put a cap on the marker.
literal
She said she could run a mile in five minutes, cap.
She said she could run a mile in five minutes, that is a lie.
slang

notes: literal with low intensity or subdued vs slang modestly or secretly (to a degree)
examples: The restaurant has a low-key atmosphere.
The restaurant has a low-key atmosphere.
literal
I'm low-key excited about the trip.
I am quietly or secretly excited about the trip.
slang

notes: literal with bright intensity vs slang openly or obviously (emphatic)
examples: The lights in the studio are high-key for the shoot.
The lights in the studio are high-key for the shoot.
literal
I'm high-key nervous about the interview.
I am openly and obviously nervous about the interview.
slang

notes: literal area out of sunlight vs slang subtle insult or criticism
examples: We sat in the shade to avoid the sun.
We sat in the shade to avoid the sun.
literal
He threw shade at her comment.
He subtly insulted her comment.
slang

notes: literal to scorch or feel heat vs slang an insulting remark or criticism
examples: Don't burn the toast.
Don't burn the toast.
literal
She made a burn about his outfit.
She made a cutting remark about his outfit.
slang

notes: literal to bend/muscle vs slang to show off or boast
examples: Flex your arm to show the muscle.
Flex your arm to show the muscle.
literal
He's flexing his new watch on social media.
He is showing off his new watch on social media.
slang

notes: literal to cook over heat vs slang to tease or severely criticize someone humorously
examples: Roast the vegetables at 400°F.
Roast the vegetables at 400°F.
literal
They roasted him about his haircut.
They made fun of him about his haircut.
slang

notes: literal clap followed by return? vs slang respond to criticism swiftly (phrase)
examples: He gave a clap back at the performance.
He gave a clap back at the performance.
literal
She clapped back at the online critic.
She responded to the online critic with a sharp reply.
slang

notes: literal drinking vessel or face vs slang to rob someone or to make a face
examples: He drank coffee from a ceramic mug.
He drank coffee from a ceramic mug.
literal
He got mugged walking home last week.
He was robbed while walking home last week.
slang

notes: literal frozen water vs slang jewelry (diamonds) or to kill (contextual)
examples: Put the ice in the cooler.
Put the ice in the cooler.
literal
She wore a lot of ice to the party.
She wore a lot of diamond jewelry to the party.
slang

notes: literal very fierce or wild vs slang ruthlessly impressive or brutally honest/praiseworthy
examples: A savage storm hit the coast.
A savage storm hit the coast.
literal
That comeback was savage.
That comeback was ruthlessly impressive.
slang

notes: literal thrown away or destroyed vs slang extremely intoxicated or ruined
examples: The food was wasted after the picnic.
The food was wasted after the picnic.
literal
He was wasted after the party.
He was extremely intoxicated after the party.
slang

notes: literal physically at a point vs slang exactly right or excellent
examples: Place the arrow on point before shooting.
Place the arrow on point before shooting.
literal
Her presentation was on point.
Her presentation was exactly right and excellent.
slang

notes: literal fruit liquid vs slang influence, energy, or advantage
examples: She squeezed the orange to get fresh juice.
She squeezed the orange to get fresh juice.
literal
He has a lot of juice in the company.
He has a lot of influence in the company.
slang

notes: literal piles vs slang large amounts of money
examples: There are stacks of books on the table.
There are stacks of books on the table.
literal
He came in with stacks after the promotion.
He had a lot of money after the promotion.
slang

notes: literal to move by turning vs slang to leave or to party (contextual)
examples: Roll the dough flat before baking.
Roll the dough flat before baking.
literal
We should roll to the next venue.
We should leave and go to the next venue.
slang

notes: literal to sit on or be carried by a vehicle vs slang to support someone or be impressive (contextual)
examples: She will ride the bus to work.
She will ride the bus to work.
literal
I'll ride with you on that decision.
I'll support you on that decision.
slang

notes: literal stone or to sway vs slang to wear confidently or be excellent
examples: A large rock blocked the path.
A large rock blocked the path.
literal
You really rock that jacket.
You wear that jacket very confidently and it looks great on you.
slang

notes: literal to present food or deliver vs slang to perform exceptionally, especially in fashion or performance
examples: They serve dinner at seven each night.
They serve dinner at seven each night.
literal
She served looks on the runway.
She presented impressive outfits on the runway.
slang

notes: literal farm animal vs slang greatest of all time
examples: The shepherd fed the goat in the morning.
The shepherd fed the goat in the morning.
literal
She is the GOAT of that sport.
She is the greatest of all time in that sport.
slang

notes: literal warmth vs slang pressure from authorities or rivalry/intensity
examples: Turn down the heat on the stove.
Turn down the heat on the stove.
literal
The team brought the heat in the second half.
The team increased their intensity/performance in the second half.
slang

notes: literal visible vapor or to inhale tobacco vs slang to decisively defeat someone
examples: Smoke rose from the chimney.
Smoke rose from the chimney.
literal
They smoked the opposing team last night.
They defeated the opposing team decisively last night.
slang

notes: literal strike a door or hit vs slang to criticize or find fault
examples: Please knock before entering the room.
Please knock before entering the room.
literal
Don't knock her hard work.
Don't criticize her hard work.
slang

notes: literal reduce to small particles vs slang to work persistently and hard
examples: Use the grinder to grind the coffee beans.
Use the grinder to grind the coffee beans.
literal
She's on the grind to finish her thesis.
She is working persistently to finish her thesis.
slang

notes: literal beverage vs slang gossip or news
examples: Would you like some tea with your snack?
Would you like some tea with your snack?
literal
Spill the tea about what happened at the party.
Tell me the gossip about what happened at the party.
slang

notes: literal a blow or strike historically vs slang influence, power, or social fame
examples: Historically, clout can mean a heavy blow.
Historically, clout can mean a heavy blow.
literal
She has a lot of clout on social media.
She has a lot of influence on social media.
slang

notes: literal awakened vs slang politically aware or conscious of injustice
examples: He woke early to catch the train.
He woke early to catch the train.
literal
She is woke about environmental issues.
She is aware and informed about environmental issues.
slang

notes: literal fundamental vs slang unoriginal or mainstream (derogatory)
examples: Understanding the basic rules helps beginners.
Understanding the basic rules helps beginners.
literal
That outfit is so basic.
That outfit is unoriginal and mainstream.
slang

notes: literal to suspend vs slang to spend time socializing (hang out)
examples: Hang the picture on that nail.
Hang the picture on that nail.
literal
Do you want to hang later?
Do you want to spend time together later?
slang

notes: literal to move smoothly along a surface vs slang to approach privately or arrive casually (e.g., 'slide into DMs' or 'slide through')
examples: The book will slide off the shelf if you push it.
The book will slide off the shelf if you push it.
literal
He slid into her messages last night.
He sent her a private direct message last night.
slang

notes: literal inflated by a pump vs slang very excited or motivated
examples: Pump the tire until it's firm.
Pump the tire until it's firm.
literal
I'm pumped for the concert tonight.
I'm very excited for the concert tonight.
slang

notes: literal apply pressure or printing press vs slang media or to pressure someone socially
examples: Press the button to start the machine.
Press the button to start the machine.
literal
He's getting a lot of press for his new film.
He is receiving a lot of media attention for his new film.
slang

notes: literal to present food vs slang to deliver or execute something effectively (lines, looks)
examples: She will serve up dinner at seven.
She will serve up dinner at seven.
literal
He served up a perfect speech at the meeting.
He delivered an excellent speech at the meeting.
slang

notes: literal hard outer covering vs slang to pay a large amount of money (shell out)
examples: The turtle retreated into its shell.
The turtle retreated into its shell.
literal
We had to shell out for new tires.
We had to pay a large amount for new tires.
slang

notes: literal to let liquid fall vs slang to reveal gossip or information
examples: Be careful not to spill the soup.
Be careful not to spill the soup.
literal
He spilled the details about the surprise party.
He revealed the details about the surprise party.
slang

notes: literal connected to power vs slang well-informed or socially connected
examples: Make sure the lamp is plugged in.
Make sure the lamp is plugged in.
literal
She's really plugged in to the tech scene.
She is well-informed and connected in the tech scene.
slang

notes: literal examine or a bill vs slang to verify or to show someone up (contextual)
examples: Check the oil level before driving.
Check the oil level before driving.
literal
Check his claim; I don't believe it.
Verify his claim; I do not believe it.
slang

notes: literal literally losing grip vs slang being caught off-guard or making a mistake
examples: He caught the rope to avoid slipping.
He caught the rope to avoid slipping.
literal
He got caught slipping during the presentation.
He was caught off-guard during the presentation.
slang

notes: literal to sell all inventory vs slang betray principles for money or fame
examples: The concert tickets sold out in minutes.
The concert tickets sold out in minutes.
literal
They sold out when they changed their music for money.
They compromised their principles for financial gain.
slang

notes: literal figurative extreme loyalty/vehicle idiom vs slang extremely loyal supporter (phrase)
examples: She is a true ride or die in every story I read.
She is a true ride or die in every story I read.
literal
He's my ride or die; he'll always support me.
He is my extremely loyal supporter; he'll always support me.
slang

notes: literal push into liquid or basketball move vs slang to decisively outperform or humiliate someone
examples: Please dunk the cookie in your milk.
Please dunk the cookie in your milk.
literal
He dunked on his rival in the debate.
He decisively outperformed his rival in the debate.
slang

notes: literal low temperature vs slang emotionally distant or impressive (e.g., 'cold' performance)
examples: The water is cold from the tap.
The water is cold from the tap.
literal
His comeback was cold and effective.
His comeback was emotionally blunt and effective.
slang

notes: literal exposed shoulder in cold vs slang deliberate unfriendliness or ignoring someone (phrase)
examples: She felt the cold shoulder of the wind.
She felt the cold shoulder of the wind.
literal
They gave him the cold shoulder after the argument.
They deliberately ignored him after the argument.
slang

notes: literal closely woven vs slang extremely close group or community
examples: The fabric is tight-knit and warm.
The fabric is tight-knit and warm.
literal
They are a tight-knit group of friends.
They are a very close group of friends.
slang

notes: literal to explode or make a popping sound vs slang to speak angrily or interruptively or excel loudly
examples: The lid will pop off if pressure builds inside.
The lid will pop off if pressure builds inside.
literal
He popped off at the meeting about the mistake.
He spoke angrily at the meeting about the mistake.
slang

notes: literal physically rotated or increased vs slang excited, lively, or partying intensely
examples: He turned up the volume on the radio.
He turned up the volume on the radio.
literal
The crowd really turned up for the headline act.
The crowd became very excited and energetic for the headline act.
slang

notes: literal hardware term vs slang ready-to-use or easy to adopt (phrase)
examples: This device is truly plug and play with the system.
This device is truly plug and play with the system.
literal
The new process was plug and play for the team.
The new process was ready-to-use and easy for the team to adopt.
slang

notes: literal device failure from overheating vs slang extreme exhaustion from overwork
examples: The circuit suffered a burnout from excessive current.
The circuit suffered a burnout from excessive current.
literal
After months of overtime she experienced burnout.
After months of overtime she experienced extreme exhaustion from overwork.
slang

notes: literal able to bend vs slang adaptable socially or willing to compromise (used informally)
examples: The material is flexible and will not break easily.
The material is flexible and will not break easily.
literal
I'm flexible on meeting times if you prefer morning.
I can adapt the meeting time if you prefer morning.
slang

notes: literal subsequent stage vs slang exceptionally improved or extraordinary
examples: We will test the prototype at the next level.
We will test the prototype at the next level.
literal
Their performance was next level.
Their performance was exceptionally impressive.
slang

notes: literal end of a joke vs slang the most impactful part of a story or message
examples: The comedian paused before the punchline.
The comedian paused before the punchline.
literal
The article saved the best point for the punchline.
The article saved the most impactful point for the end.
slang

notes: literal facade or front of a building vs slang to pretend or present a false image
examples: Stand in front of the house for the photo.
Stand in front of the house for the photo.
literal
He's fronting like he knows more than he does.
He is pretending to know more than he does.
slang

notes: literal connect to power vs slang integrate or include someone into a group or system
examples: Plug in the laptop to charge it.
Plug in the laptop to charge it.
literal
He was quickly plugged in to the local scene.
He was quickly integrated into the local scene.
slang

notes: literal small military unit vs slang close group of friends or associates
examples: The squad marched through the training ground.
The squad marched through the training ground.
literal
I'm going out with my squad tonight.
I'm going out with my close group of friends tonight.
slang

notes: literal physically board vs slang to begin or succeed at something or to date someone (contextual)
examples: Get on the bus before it leaves.
Get on the bus before it leaves.
literal
She is trying to get on in her career.
She is trying to make progress in her career.
slang

notes: literal to lift or support vs slang to delay or to rob (contextual)
examples: Hold up the sign so everyone can see it.
Hold up the sign so everyone can see it.
literal
Sorry I'm late, the traffic held me up.
Sorry I'm late, the traffic delayed me.
slang

notes: literal secure something in place vs slang manage a situation or remain responsible
examples: Make sure to hold it down while I leave the load.
Make sure to hold it down while I leave the load.
literal
Can you hold it down at the office while I'm away?
Can you manage things at the office while I'm away?
slang

notes: literal start media playback vs slang begin an activity or proceed with an idea
examples: Press play to start the video.
Press play to start the video.
literal
Let's press play on the project tomorrow.
Let's begin the project tomorrow.
slang

notes: literal utensil for serving vs slang exclusive information or to outperform someone in news
examples: Use a scoop to serve the ice cream.
Use a scoop to serve the ice cream.
literal
They got the scoop on the story before anyone else.
They obtained exclusive information about the story before anyone else.
slang

notes: literal nearest seating vs slang prime position or advantage
examples: We sat in the front row at the concert.
We sat in the front row at the concert.
literal
She has a front-row seat to the industry changes.
She has a prime position to observe the industry changes.
slang

notes: literal to let fall vs slang to release (song, product) or fail (contextual)
examples: Don't drop the fragile vase.
Don't drop the fragile vase.
literal
The band will drop a new single next week.
The band will release a new single next week.
slang

notes: literal excessive in quantity vs slang overly dramatic or intense behavior
examples: There's too much salt in the soup.
There's too much salt in the soup.
literal
Her reaction to the joke was too much.
Her reaction to the joke was overly dramatic.
slang
